coredata.dc:title: Reduction of Congo red using nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon nanodots obtained from sprout extract of Borassus flabellifer
coredata.dc:creator: Arul, V.
Chandrasekaran, P.
Sethuraman, M.G.
coredata.dc:description: Here, we have demonstrated a facile hydrothermal carbonization pathway for the fabrication of luminescent nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs). The sprout extract of Borassus flabellifer (B. flabellifer) and aquous ammonia (aq.NH3) were utilized as a source of carbon and nitrogen, respectively. Using diverse microscopic and spectroscopic methods, the fabricated N-CNDs were characterized and reported. The excitation dependant emission and absorbance features of the prepared N-CNDs were studied using fluorescence and UV–Visible spectroscopic methods. When excited at 320 nm the obtained N-CNDs exposed deep blue emission at 405 nm. The quantum yield (QY) for the synthesized N-CNDs was found to be 41%. The HR-TEM results confirmed the spherical shape and the size of the prepared N-CNDs to be 2.37 nm. The amorphous character of the synthesized N-CNDs was revealed by SAED pattern, XRD and Raman spectroscopic studies. Doping of nitrogen over surface of the CNDs was examined and confirmed through FT-IR, XPS and EDS analyses. XPS and FT-IR studies also exposed the presence of –COOH, NH2, OH and CO functional groups over the N-CNDs surface. The synthesized N-CNDs were found to have profound catalytic effect on the reduction of congo red by NaBH4. Thus the study has unravelled the nature and utility of N-CNDs from B. flabellifer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Borassus flabellifer
Nitrogen doped carbon nanodots
Catalytic effect
Congo red

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Absorbance and fluorescent spectra for the synthesized N-CNDs
Catalytic ability of N-CNDs in the reduction of CR using NaBH4

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Department of Science and Technology – Science and Engineering Research Board (DST-SERB) for the financial support for this work through a major research project on “Eco-friendly ............Catalysis” (Sanction No: EMR/2016/006024 ). The authors also place on record the encouragement from the authorities of GRI.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorbance spectra for extract and prepared N-CNDs and (b) fluorescent excitation spectra of N-CNDs.
Excitation dependent emission responses of synthesised N-CNDs at wide-ranging of excitation wavelengths from (a) 280 nm − 320 nm and (b) 320 nm − 400 nm.
(a) HR-TEM representation of N-CNDs (b) size histogram of N-CNDs (c) N-CNDs representation in higher magnification and (d) SAED outline of N-CNDs.
Fourier transform infrared spectra of B. flabellier extract and N-CNDs.
X-Ray Diffraction outline of N-CNDs from B. flabellifer.
XPS spectrum of N-CNDs (a) complete scan; high-resolution deconvolution spectrum of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.
UV–Vis absorbance spectra of the CR reduction using NaBH4 (a) without N-CNDs and (b) with N-CNDs (c) Kinetics plot for the reduction of CR with N-CNDs.
Schematic representation of reduction of congo red using NaBH4 with N-CNDs.
Comparison table of reported QY values of the CDs with synthesized N-CNDs.
Comparison table of different nanocatalysts for reducing Congo Red at 25 °C.

head.ce:title: Reduction of Congo red using nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon nanodots obtained from sprout extract of Borassus flabellifer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram, 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram, 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we have demonstrated a facile hydrothermal carbonization pathway for the fabrication of luminescent nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs). The sprout extract of Borassus flabellifer (B. flabellifer) and aquous ammonia (aq.NH3) were utilized as a source of carbon and nitrogen, respectively. Using diverse microscopic and spectroscopic methods, the fabricated N-CNDs were characterized and reported. The excitation dependant emission and absorbance features of the prepared N-CNDs were studied using fluorescence and UV–Visible spectroscopic methods. When excited at 320 nm the obtained N-CNDs exposed deep blue emission at 405 nm. The quantum yield (QY) for the synthesized N-CNDs was found to be 41%. The HR-TEM results confirmed the spherical shape and the size of the prepared N-CNDs to be 2.37 nm. The amorphous character of the synthesized N-CNDs was revealed by SAED pattern, XRD and Raman spectroscopic studies. Doping of nitrogen over surface of the CNDs was examined and confirmed through FT-IR, XPS and EDS analyses. XPS and FT-IR studies also exposed the presence of –COOH, NH2, OH and CO functional groups over the N-CNDs surface. The synthesized N-CNDs were found to have profound catalytic effect on the reduction of congo red by NaBH4. Thus the study has unravelled the nature and utility of N-CNDs from B. flabellifer.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the various nanoparticles, semiconductor quantum dots have gained more interest owing to their distinctive electronic and photoluminescent properties. Though they possess unique features, they are highly toxic as they contain heavy metals and this had led to the development of novel harmless carbon skeletal nanoparticles known as ‘carbon nanodots' [1,2]. The carbon nanodots (CNDs) are a most recent category of nanomaterials which is mainly constructed by carbon with a size between 1 and 10 nm in diameter. CNDs are better than various other semiconductor quantum dots owing to their fashionable properties i.e., high aqueous dispersibility, low cytotoxicity, resistance to photo-bleaching, high chemical stability and superior biocompatibility. The low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility of the luminescent carbon dots was the reason for their exploitation in the field of bio-imaging and drug delivery. Their discovery also triggered the extensive study on CNDs to make use of their fluorescent property in bio-sensing, catalysis and bio-medicine, energy storage and optoelectronics, etc., [3–5].

Further, CNDs are widely used as an excellent oxidizing and reducing agent due to their tremendous electron accepting and electron donating capability [6,7]. This behaviour helps to extend their applicability as a catalyst for environmental remediation through the removal of dyes, toxic pollutants, etc. The plant extracts are ingeniously utilized as carbon source to produce carbon nanodots is the major aim of this work, due to the advantages such as eco-friendly nature and cost [8–12]. The pure CNDs show a lesser amount of catalytic behaviour and low photoluminescence quantum yield when compared to heteroatom doped CNDs [13]. The catalytic activity and fluorescent behaviour are drastically enhanced due to the doping of boron, nitrogen, sulphur, and phosphorous (heteroatoms) on CNDs surface. Hence, these heteroatom doped CNDs have become efficient material for various applications such as bio-imaging, bio-sensing, electrochemical sensing, photo and electro catalytic applications [14,15].

The usage of organic dyes in textiles, food, paper, and pharmaceutical industries are quite common for production purposes [16,17]. From these industries, huge quantities of effluents containing untreated pollutants have been mixed into rivers and lakes every day. These pollutants amend the range of pH which raises the BOD (biological oxygen demand) and COD (chemical oxygen demand) levels in water bodies leading to increased environmental pollution [18]. Further, it also leads to diseases to the living beings in water and its surroundings. For maintaining safe and wealthy environment, it is very much essential to control industrial pollution. Now a days, development of easy biological techniques for the removal of dyes have gained momentum compared to other conventional methods. Congo red (CR) is an anionic azo dye, the usage of which has been discouraged due to its carcinogenic properties [19,20]. The subtraction of CR from the industrial waste water (effluents) remains a challenging assignment for scientists working in this field.

Borassus flabellifer (B. flabellifer) is commonly known as palmyra palm belonging to the family Arecaceae. The palm is found growing in southern states of India especially in Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Bihar and Orissa. They have great economic potential and every part of the palm is useful in one way or the other [21,22]. Palmyra sprout is found to be a good source of Vitamin A and C. The sap is a good source of vitamin B complex. It is also a rich source of carbohydrates like sucrose. Some essential mineral elements such as Magnesium, Iron, Zinc, Calcium and phosphorus are also present. The tuber contains 98% of fibre and 95% starch. The chemical constituents of B. flabellifer consist of fats, sucrose, albuminoids and steroidal glycosides. Pharmacological actions like anti-inflammatory, antibiotic, antibacterial, analgesic, antipyretic, hypoglycaemic and antioxidant effects have been reported for palmyra palm [23,24].

In the present study, sprouts of B. flabellifer (Palmyra) are used as source of carbon and aq·NH3 performing the role as a nitrogen dopant for the preparation of nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) by simple hydrothermal carbonization pathway. The prepared N-CNDs were studied and confirmed by the diverse spectroscopic characterization techniques. The favourable catalytic capability of the prepared N-CNDs was examined through the Congo red dye reduction using NaBH4 and the reduction reaction can be monitored by Ultra-Violet Visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopic studies.

The sprouts of B. flabellifer (Palmyra tuber) were procured at Dindigul, Tamil Nadu, India. The Congo red (CR), aq·NH3 and sodium borohydride were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. For the preparation of solutions and other uses, the double distilled water (DD) was employed.

The slashed little pieces of B. flabellifer sprouts (50–60 g) were taken into a mixer grinder along with 20–30 mL of water and crushed well. The crushed B. flabellifer extract was filtered with cotton cloth to remove the larger particles. To get transparent (clear) extract, the solution was additionally filtered by Whatmann filtration sheet (Grade No. 40). The prepared B. flabellifer extract was utilized as a carbon precursor for the hydrothermal synthesis of CNDs.

The preparation of nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) from B. flabellifer sprout extract was efficiently done by well-known hydrothermal carbonization process. The 29 mL of B. flabellifer extract and 1 mL aq·NH3 (1 M) were concurrently transmitted to autoclave having Teflon Lin with 50 mL capacity. The carbonization reaction was carried out in a hot air oven with high pressure and temperature for 12 h at 180 °C. After over hydrothermal process, the reaction vessel was carefully opened when cooled to room temperature (25 °C). The proficient formation of N-CNDs has been visually confirmed by the colour change of the extract (mild green to dark brown colour). To get pure N-CNDs, the obtained solution were carefully filtered and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 min to take away the impurities and non-reacted organic moieties. The final filtrate having the synthesized N-CNDs was stocked up at below 10 °C for subjected to characterization and applications.

For recording UV–Visible and fluorescence spectra of the synthesised N-CNDs Perkin Elmer lambda and Jasco FP 8500 fluorometer were used. The HR-TEM surface analysis of the prepared N-CNDs were done by JOEL JEM 2100 electron microscope with operating voltage of 200 kV by put in a drop of diluted N-CNDs over the copper grid (200 mesh with carbon coating) and dried up at 60 °C. The functional groups present in the extract of B. flabellifer (dried) and prepared N-CNDs were studied through FT-IR spectrometer (Jasco FT-IR 460 Plus). PANalytical XPERT X-ray Diffractometer was utilized for recording the XRD pattern of the synthesised N-CNDs. The STR Raman spectrometer with 500 mm focal length were used for recording the Raman spectra of the synthesized N-CNDs. FEI Inc - PHI 5000 Versa Probe – II X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy instrument was used for recording XPS spectra of the prepared N-CNDs. Horiba yobin (Fluorolog 3) spectrofluorometer was used to find out the quantum yield of the synthesized N-CNDs.

The Congo red (CR) reduction using NaBH4 with the help of synthesized green catalyst (N-CNDs) was monitored by UV–Visible spectrometer through 3.5 mL capacity quartz cuvette with 1 cm path length. The catalytic reaction was carried out at ambient temperature (25 °C). In the first reaction, 1 mL of the CR dye (0.1 M) was mixed with DD water (1 mL) and freshly prepared 0.2 M ice cold NaBH4 (0.5 mL). Additionally, the 50 µL of prepared green catalyst (N-CNDs) was added to the reaction mixture to carry out the second reaction. The CR reduction with and without green catalyst (N-CNDs) was monitored through UV–Visible spectroscopy with different time intervals.

The hydrothermal carbonization process was successfully carried out for the synthesis of fluorescent N-CNDs from the extract of B. flabellifer and aq·NH3. The colour change of the extract from mild green to reddish brown colour was the initial confirmation of the N-CNDs formation [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorbance and fluorescent spectra for the synthesized N-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Visible spectroscopic method is a well-known and most widely used valuable technique for exploring the optical properties of the synthesized CNDs. Fig. 1 (a) depicts the absorbance spectra for the aqueous extract of B. flabellifer sprout (carbon source) and synthesised N-CNDs. The peak appeared at 275 nm for the carbon source due to the π-π* transition, and the N-CNDs showed peaks at 270 nm and 320 nm, because of the π-π* transitions of the CC (sp2) bonds and the n-π* transitions of the non-bonding electrons present in N or CO functional groups presence in the CDs surface respectively [13,26,27]. (Fig. 1 (b)) displays the fluorescent excitation spectra for N-CNDs which is contemporaneous with the UV–Vis spectrum.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the higher magnification TEM representation of the biogenic N doped CNDs in 20 nm magnification range. The spherical shaped N-CNDs could be identified from the obtained TEM representation (Fig. 3 (a)). The mean diameter of the N-CNDs was found to be 2.37 nm with particle size distribution of 1 to 5 nm which was shown in the particle size histogram (Fig. 3 (b)). Fig. 3 (c) displays the higher magnification TEM representation of the N-CNDs at 5 nm. Amorphous phase of the N-CNDs was identified by the formation of clear circles in selected area energy dispersion pattern (Fig. 3 (d)) carbon [25–27].

The EDS spectra (Fig. 4 ) provide exact details about the elements containing the synthesized N-CNDs such as C (74.39 %), N (8.49 %), and O (17.2 %) which clearly denoted the functionalization of the synthesized N-CNDs with nitrogen atoms [40,41].

XRD pattern for the N-CNDs is shown in Fig. 6 . The XRD peaks for N-CNDs appeared at 2θ values of 28.4° for C(0 0 2) plane and 40.4° for C(1 0 0) plane of the graphitic carbon respectively [41,42]. Further, the two weak peaks which appeared at (2θ) 43.2° and 54.5° were due to C(1 0 1) and C (0 0 4) planes, respectively [43,44]. The inter layer distance (d-spacing) value for N-CNDs was found out using the Bragg’s equation.

The XRD peak could have appeared as broad due to the tiny sized N-CNDs. The calculated d-spacing (inter layer distance) values were 0.314 nm for C(0 0 2) plane and 0.222 nm for C(1 0 0) plane, respectively. These results could be correlated to the interlayer distance (d- spacing) of the lattice fringes present in the HR-TEM images (Fig. 3 (c)) [43]. The XRD results thus clearly indicated that the structure of the synthesized N-CNDs was modestly graphitic in nature [45,46].

For characterization of the carbon nanostructures, Raman spectroscopy is the most valuable method. Fig. 7 , depicts the Raman spectrum for the N-CNDs. From the obtained spectrum, we observed a small and broad band at 1349 cm−1 and 1594 cm−1 respectively, which indicated the D band and G bands of the synthesized N-CNDs. The D and G bands arise due to the defects appearing on graphitic layers and vibrations of the sp2 carbon in the graphitic structure of the N-CNDs, respectively [43,47]. For determining graphitization of the synthesized N-CNDs, the calculation of intensity ratio between the D and G bands (ID/IG) is very much important (ID/IG < 1 for ideal graphite) [48]. The calculated ID/IG ratio for N-CNDs is 0.22 which indicates the moderate graphite structure of the prepared N-CNDs. From the above discussion, it is confirmed that the obtained N-CNDs contains negligible amount of non-reacted organic moieties [43].

The surface electronic states for the N-CNDs were examined by recording XPS spectra (Fig. 8 ). The X-ray photo electron spectrum (XPS) of N-CNDs (Fig. 8. (a)) displays three distinctive peaks of C1s (282.8 eV), N1s (397.8 eV) and O1s (530.5 eV). The deconvoluted C1s spectrum (Fig. 8. (b)) provides fours peaks viz., at 282.3 eV for C-H groups, 283.7 eV for CC (sp2 carbon), 285.2 eV for C-N bonds and 290.3 eV for π-π* transitions [49–51]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 8 (c)) can be deconvoluted into two peaks which appeared at 398.1, and 397.15 eV due to the amine and amide functionalities present in the N-CNDs surface. The deconvoluted O1s (Fig. 8 (d)) displays two main peaks at 528.6 eV for COC and 529.8 eV for CO/NO bonds [4,25,52]. From the XPS spectral studies, we concluded that the surface of the CNDs was effectively doped by nitrogen atom. The elemental percentage of the synthesized N-CNDs was C (74.45%), N (8.54%), and O (17.01%) which corroborates to the EDS results. Moreover, the XPS results also evidenced for the hydroxyl, amine and carboxyl groups present in the surface of the N-CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic ability of N-CNDs in the reduction of CR using NaBH4
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of catalytic rate constant (Kcat) for CR reduction reaction by green synthesized N-CNDs could be done based on the previously reported work using psuedo first order kinectics [55,56]. Fig. 9 (c) shows the rate constant linear plot between change in absorbance (ln (At/A0)) and reaction time (T). The rate constant (k) and coefficient (R2) were obtained from the slope values of the linear plot. The catalytic rate constant (Kcat) and R2 values were found to be 0.3146 min−1 and 0.9767, respectively. Chandrasekaran et al., has reported the catalytic reduction of methyl orange using nitrogen doped CDs and Kcat value of the N-CDs was found to be 0.12636 min−1 [55]. Dhenadhayalan et al., had reported the Kcat value of 0.0265 s−1 for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol by Pd doped CDs [56]. The obtained Kcat value for the CR reduction using N-CNDs was quite higher than the previous reports. Moreover, the plot of ln (At/A0) Vs T was not linear for CR reduction reaction carried out using only NaBH4 without green catalyst.

This catalytic reduction of CR can be explained by well–known Langmuir Hinshelwood kinetics mechanism [35]. The synthesised N-CNDs served as a mediator for transmiting the electrons from BH4 - ions (donor) to the CR (acceptor) [4,24,41]. The activation energy is lowered thus increasing the reduction rate of the dye [57–62]. Fig. 10 . shows the schematic representation of reduction of congo red using NaBH4 with N-CNDs. The obtained final products in the reduction of CR by NaBH4 using synthesized N-CNDs were 1,1′-biphenyl and sodium salt of 4-amino-1-naphthalene sulfonic acid [63]. The toxic CR dye was converted into environmentally begnin products through eco-friendly green catalyst. The proficient catalytic ability of the synthesized N-CNDs is the better alternative for toxic metal nanocatalysts in the effective removal of environmental pollutions [64].

The green carbonization of N-CNDs was successfully completed by efficient hydrothermal route with B. flabellifer as green carbon source and aq·NH3 as doping agent of nitrogen. The absorbance and emission properties were examined by UV–Visible spectroscopy and fluorescence spectrofluorometer. HR-TEM studies proved the quasi-spherical morphology of the N-CNDs and the size of N-CNDs was found to be 2.37 nm in diameter. The IR, EDS and XPS studies confirmed that the nitrogen was successfully doped on the CNDs surface. XRD pattern confirmed the amorphous character of the N-CNDs. It could be seen from the results that the biogenic N-CNDs have the ability to enhance the catalytic effect of NaBH4 in the reduction of Congo red. The complete reduction could be brought about in 10 min.
Thus, the study shows that facile preparation of N-CNDs can be made from B. flabellifer as the green source and the prepared N-CNDs can be used for the reduction of Congo Red.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Department of Science and Technology – Science and Engineering Research Board (DST-SERB) for the financial support for this work through a major research project on “Eco-friendly ............Catalysis” (Sanction No: EMR/2016/006024). The authors also place on record the encouragement from the authorities of GRI.
